topic: best place to buy chicken in bulk

turns.content: help me na ka I need advice about buying chicken!! so my usual chicken vendor at the market raised her price AGAIN and I need to figure out if I should switch or negotiate or what 😩

Price increases are always stressful, especially when chicken is your biggest ingredient cost! How much did the price go up, and do you have a sense of why — is it a supply issue, or just her margins going up?

she raise by 3 baht per kilo na ka. I know it sound small but I buy A LOT of chicken every day so it add up fast. she say the farm price go up because of feed cost. I don't know if she is telling truth or just taking more profit haha. but I cannot really argue because she has been my supplier for years

A 3 baht increase per kilo definitely adds up at your volume. Feed costs have genuinely been going up in Thailand recently, so she might be telling the truth — but it's always worth checking around.

Have you looked at any other suppliers or wholesale markets? Sometimes comparing prices gives you leverage even if you don't switch.

that is what I am thinking na ka. I know a few place I could check. there is Talat Thai wholesale which is big but SO far from my area, like in Pathum Thani. and then there is Or Tor Kor market which is closer but more expensive because it's the fancy market. and some people tell me to buy direct from the farm but I don't know any farm

Let me break down the trade-offs for each:

**Talat Thai (Pathum Thani):**
- Biggest wholesale market, best prices usually
- But the drive is long — you'd burn time and fuel getting there at 4am
- Worth it if the savings per kilo are significant at your volume

**Or Tor Kor:**
- Closer and convenient, quality is excellent
- But prices are premium since it caters to a higher-end crowd
- Probably not the answer if you're trying to save money

**Direct from farm:**
- Can be cheapest, cuts out the middleman
- But you need a reliable farm, consistent quality, and a way to arrange regular delivery
- Harder to set up but great long-term if you can find the right one

What's your current vendor's location? Is switching mainly about price or are there quality concerns too?

my vendor is at my regular morning market, so very convenient. the quality is good na ka, I never complain about the quality. it's just the price keep going up. 6 months ago she raise 2 baht, now another 3 baht. if this continue every 6 months I cannot absorb the cost without raising my plate price and I don't want to do that

That's 5 baht per kilo increase in 6 months — at your daily volume, that's a meaningful hit to your margins. I understand not wanting to raise plate prices because in street food, customers are very price-sensitive.

Here's what I'd suggest:

1. **Visit Talat Thai once** — go on a Sunday morning just to compare prices and see the quality. Get contacts from a few vendors. Even if you don't switch, knowing the wholesale price gives you a benchmark.
2. **Talk to your current vendor** — tell her honestly that the increases are adding up and ask if she can hold the price for the next 6 months. Long-term loyal customers have negotiating power.
3. **Ask around in the vendor community** — other street food sellers who buy chicken in bulk probably have supplier tips. Someone might know a farm connection.

Would any of those work for your schedule?

hmm going to Talat Thai on Sunday is actually smart because that is my day off na ka!! I can go check and compare. and yes I should ask other vendors. there is a pad ka prao lady near my spot who buy chicken too, maybe she know someone good. I think the negotiation approach is best first though — I don't want to lose my relationship with my vendor because she is reliable, she know exactly what cuts I need

That's the right approach — negotiate first, explore alternatives second. Your current vendor knowing exactly what you need is worth a lot. Reliability and consistency matter more than saving a couple of baht if the new supplier gives you headaches.

When you talk to her, you could say something like: "I've been buying from you for years and I want to keep buying from you, but the increases are making it hard for me. Can we work something out?" Framing it as wanting to stay loyal usually gets a better response than threatening to leave.

And the Talat Thai visit is good insurance — even if you don't switch, you'll know what prices look like elsewhere. Knowledge is power in negotiation.

okay good plan na ka!! I will talk to her tomorrow morning. and this Sunday I go to Talat Thai just to look around. thank you for helping me think this through. my brain was just panicking about the price haha but now I feel like I have a strategy. every baht matters when you sell plates at my price 💪

Every baht absolutely matters — that's the street food game. Small margins, big volume, so any cost increase hits hard. But you've got a solid plan now: negotiate first, scout alternatives second, and you'll make a smart decision either way.

Good luck with the conversation tomorrow morning! Be friendly but firm. And enjoy the Talat Thai trip on Sunday — even if you don't find a new supplier, wholesale markets are kind of fun to explore. Let me know how it goes! 💪

will do na ka!! I update you after. who knows maybe I find some amazing deal at Talat Thai and start going every Sunday haha. okay time to go prep for tomorrow — chicken is not going to marinate itself 😂 thank you again!! 🙏

Haha, the chicken definitely won't marinate itself! Go get that prep done. And hey, even if Talat Thai ends up being too far for a regular run, at least you'll know the prices — that info alone is worth the trip. Talk soon and good luck tomorrow! 🙏
haha yes knowledge is power na ka!! okay I go now for real. next time I talk to you I will be a chicken negotiation expert 😂💪 sawatdee ka!!
Chicken negotiation expert — love it! Go crush it. Sawatdee ka! 💪
